name: Context Engineering
scope: A person with INTERMEDIATE proficiency level in Context Engineering as a competency should be able to design and structure context for LLM systems, distinguishing between model knowledge and runtime context (prompts, retrieved data, user/session state, tool outputs), and understanding context window/tokenization limits. They can analyze business problems to define context requirements, perform task decomposition, select appropriate retrieval and grounding strategies (including vector/hybrid search, chunking, filtering, embeddings, RAG pipelines), and construct prompt templates with clear separation of instructions, user input, retrieved evidence, and tool descriptions. They are adept at configuring and using tools like vector databases, search engines, LLM orchestration frameworks (e.g., LangChain, LlamaIndex), and embedding libraries, and can manage context pipelines that integrate with enterprise data sources and APIs. They apply context modeling techniques (schemas, metadata, chunking), design memory/state management for multi-turn interactions, and implement context-aware caching, summarization, and token management strategies. They can evaluate context quality using metrics (groundedness, recall, precision, hallucination rate), set up evaluation datasets and use LLM-based or heuristic evaluation tools. They understand and apply safety, privacy, and access control principles (PII redaction, tenant isolation, prompt injection resistance), and document context pipeline architecture, schemas, and prompt templates. They debug and analyze context-related failures using logs, traces, and prompt inspection, and collaborate with stakeholders to validate context use. They automate common context engineering tasks, manage lifecycle/versioning of context artifacts, and participate in code/design reviews, knowledge sharing, and iterative experimentation for context pipeline improvements.
long_scope:
{"sections":[{"name":"Core Fundamentals of Context in AI Systems","scope_included":"- Understands context as all information influencing model behavior at inference time, including prompts, retrieved data, tool outputs, user/session state, configuration and policies.
- Differentiates clearly between model capabilities from pretraining/fine-tuning and what is supplied as runtime context (grounding data, tools, instructions).
- Distinguishes and works with different layers of context:
  - System / policy / guardrail instructions
  - Task instructions and constraints
  - User input (current turn and prior turns)
  - Retrieved and external knowledge
  - Application-specific state and metadata
- Understands the limits of context windows and how tokenization affects what can be provided to the model.
- Explains how context quality, relevance, recency, and structure affect:
  - Hallucination vs groundedness
  - Faithfulness to source data
  - Task success rate
  - Latency and cost
- Understands different types of context:
  - Static reference material vs dynamic transactional data
  - Global vs per-tenant vs per-user context
  - Short-term (session) vs long-term (persistent) memory
- Recognizes the difference between instruction-following and knowledge-grounding, and how each is represented in context.
- Understands at a working level how tokenization, truncation, and model input formatting (chat vs completion APIs, function/tool calls) interact with context design.
- Understands the relationship between context engineering, prompt engineering, retrieval systems, and application architecture, and can place their work within that ecosystem.","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Problem Framing and Context Requirements Analysis","scope_included":"- Translates business problems and user needs into concrete context requirements:
  - What the model must know
  - What it must never assume
  - What must be strictly enforced as constraints
- Performs task decomposition to identify which subtasks require which kinds of context (knowledge, rules, history, user attributes, tools).
- Identifies information gaps and determines whether they should be addressed via:
  - Additional retrieval
  - Additional user questions
  - Precomputation or preprocessing
  - Non-LLM logic
- Defines explicit success criteria for context usage (groundedness, coverage, allowed sources, traceability, citations).
- Chooses appropriate context strategies for common task types:
  - Question answering over documents or knowledge bases
  - Summarization and synthesis across multiple sources
  - Data extraction / information extraction
  - Workflow assistance / copilots
  - Code assistance and configuration guidance
- Distinguishes when LLM + context is appropriate versus when deterministic or traditional IR/ML solutions are preferable.
- Identifies domain-specific constraints that shape context (regulation, confidentiality, internal terminology, criticality of correctness).","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Context Modeling, Structuring, and Representation","scope_included":"- Designs structured representations of context suited to the domain and tasks, including schemas for documents, entities, and interactions.
- Defines and uses metadata and attributes (source, timestamp, tenant, permissions, content type, language, confidence, version) to control retrieval and inclusion.
- Applies chunking strategies for unstructured content:
  - Fixed-size vs semantic vs structure-aware chunking
  - Overlap windows and continuity
  - Trade-offs between chunk size, recall, and precision
- Structures context into well-separated sections within prompts (instructions, retrieved evidence, examples, constraints, tool descriptions) using clear delimiters and labeling.
- Chooses appropriate representation formats:
  - Plain text vs markdown vs semi-structured formats (JSON, YAML, XML)
  - Tabular vs narrative vs hierarchical representations
- Designs context layouts that support:
  - Easy citation and attribution
  - Disambiguation between multiple sources
  - Explicit separation of facts vs policies vs examples vs user data
- Uses lightweight ontologies or taxonomies (even if informal) to standardize entities, relations, and terminology within context.
- Understands and applies representation techniques for structured data:
  - Instructional schemas for tables
  - Serializing structured records into consistent textual formats
  - Including compact summaries of large structures when full inclusion is infeasible.
- Designs context to be robust against minor changes in source data (schema evolution, new document types) without constant prompt redesign.","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Retrieval and Grounding Mechanisms (RAG and Beyond)","scope_included":"- Designs and implements retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines suitable for typical enterprise/documental use cases.
- Constructs and maintains indexes over heterogeneous data:
  - Text corpora (documents, tickets, emails, knowledge articles)
  - Semi-structured data (logs, tables, configs)
  - Code repositories
- Uses and configures vector search and hybrid search systems:
  - Vector databases or embeddings over relational / document stores
  - Hybrid lexical + semantic retrieval (BM25 + embeddings, etc.)
  - Basic reranking strategies to improve relevance
- Selects embeddings and controls their usage:
  - Chooses embedding models appropriate to language/domain and cost
  - Understands dimensionality, index types, and approximate nearest neighbor search
  - Manages embedding lifecycle and re-embedding strategies when content or models change
- Implements filtering and scoping:
  - Security and access control filters
  - Tenant / user / project scoping
  - Time-based and freshness filters
  - Content-type or quality filters
- Configures retrieval parameters and hyperparameters (top-k, similarity thresholds, score normalization) and understands their impact on:
  - Recall vs precision
  - Context length vs noise
  - Latency and cost
- Designs grounding flows:
  - Combining results from multiple indices or sources
  - Normalizing, deduplicating, and ranking retrieved chunks
  - Interleaving retrieved evidence with instructions and user input
- Applies strategies to mitigate hallucinations through context:
  - Emphasizing authoritative sources
  - Including explicit instructions about when to say "unknown"
  - Including counterexamples or negative evidence when relevant
- Understands and applies basic multi-hop or query transformation approaches:
  - Query rewriting / expansion prior to retrieval
  - Decomposing complex questions into simpler retrieval steps
  - Using the model itself to refine or generate retrieval queries, with appropriate guardrails.
- Designs context-aware caching strategies (query results, retrieved sets) to improve performance while respecting freshness and permissions.","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Prompt and Instruction Design within Context Pipelines","scope_included":"- Designs layered prompts that separate and prioritize:
  - System/policy instructions
  - Task instructions
  - User input
  - Retrieved context
  - Examples / demonstrations
  - Tool / function descriptions
- Constructs robust prompt templates with clear placeholders and formatting rules, avoiding brittle string concatenation and ambiguity.
- Uses established prompting patterns in context-rich systems:
  - Retrieval-then-answer with explicit citation sections
  - Read-then-transform (summarize, rewrite, extract)
  - Plan-and-execute workflows with intermediate reasoning steps (with or without visible chain-of-thought)
  - Tools-first or context-first strategies based on task type
- Designs instructions that:
  - Force grounding in provided context
  - Distinguish between when to use retrieved data vs general knowledge
  - Control tone, style, and format consistently
  - Enforce constraints (schemas, allowed operations, response formats)
- Controls chain-of-thought exposure appropriately:
  - Uses hidden reasoning where needed for quality
  - Ensures sensitive reasoning is not exposed to end users where inappropriate
- Designs few-shot examples and demonstrations that complement retrieved context without contradicting it.
- Handles tool / function calling instructions:
  - Describes tools, inputs, outputs, and guarantees clearly for the model
  - Structures prompts so the model knows when to call tools vs answer directly
  - Aligns tool responses with subsequent context integration
- Designs prompts resilient to prompt injection within retrieved or user-provided content by:
  - Clearly delimiting untrusted content
  - Reiterating security and policy instructions after untrusted sections
  - Using structured, model-enforced formats where possible.
- Iteratively refines prompts based on observed failure modes and evaluation results, using systematic prompt change management rather than ad-hoc tweaks.","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Multi-turn Interaction, Memory, and State Management","scope_included":"- Designs strategies for carrying forward relevant context across turns without uncontrolled context growth.
- Differentiates and applies:
  - Stateless single-turn designs
  - Session-based conversational memory
  - Persistent user- or entity-level profiles
- Implements memory mechanisms:
  - Conversation summarization and abstraction into compact representations
  - Storing and retrieving prior turns with semantic search
  - Maintaining explicit state objects (forms, workflows, task states) outside the model, and passing them back as context
- Defines which elements of past interaction are important to retain (goals, preferences, constraints, unresolved issues) and which to discard.
- Designs summarization prompts specific to memory purposes vs user-facing summarization (e.g., preserving decision-critical details vs surface-level recap).
- Handles reference resolution, ellipsis, and pronouns by providing sufficient prior context or structured state ("that invoice", "the second item", "the previous error").
- Implements and tunes forgetting and expiry policies for memory to control:
  - Privacy and data retention
  - Performance and context window usage
  - Relevance over time
- Handles context resets, session restarts, and error recovery paths that re-establish necessary context efficiently.
- Designs flows where important long-term facts (user attributes, system configurations, domain constraints) are stored in durable stores and selectively surfaced as context when relevant.","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Evaluation, Metrics, and Quality Control for Context Use","scope_included":"- Designs evaluation plans that distinguish between model quality and context quality.
- Defines and applies metrics relevant to context engineering, such as:
  - Groundedness / faithfulness to provided context
  - Context coverage and recall (did we retrieve/include what was needed?)
  - Context precision (amount of irrelevant or distracting information)
  - Hallucination rate
  - Answer correctness / usefulness for target tasks
  - Format adherence and constraint compliance
- Sets up and maintains evaluation datasets that include:
  - Queries/questions with known correct answers and supporting references
  - Edge cases that stress context limits (ambiguous, sparse, conflicting, or long documents)
- Conducts manual evaluation with structured rubrics and guidelines, particularly focusing on whether outputs are correctly grounded in context.
- Uses automated and semi-automated evaluation tools and frameworks for RAG and LLM evaluation, including:
  - LLM-based judges for relevance and groundedness with proper calibration
  - Heuristic checks (citation presence, schema compliance, sanity checks)
- Designs regression tests for context pipelines so that changes to retrieval, prompting, or context structure do not silently degrade quality.
- Analyzes failure cases by breaking them down into:
  - Retrieval failures
  - Context selection/truncation failures
  - Prompt/instruction failures
  - Model inference failures
- Uses experiment results to guide systematic changes in retrieval configuration, chunking, prompt design, or context formats rather than random tuning.
- Understands and applies basic statistical interpretation of A/B test results and evaluation scores (variance, significance, sample size considerations).","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Optimization, Token Management, and Trade-offs","scope_included":"- Analyzes and manages context size relative to model limits and cost constraints.
- Designs token budgeting strategies:
  - Allocating tokens among instructions, retrieved content, memory, and examples
  - Prioritizing high-value context when limits are approached
- Implements truncation and selection strategies:
  - Ranking and filtering retrieved chunks beyond naive top-k
  - Collapsing or summarizing low-importance sections
  - Focusing on most relevant document sections or fields
- Applies summarization and compression techniques to large contexts:
  - Pre-summarizing long documents into multi-level summaries
  - On-the-fly summarization of retrieved sets before final prompting
  - Combining extracted key facts with links to full content
- Balances latency vs quality:
  - Choosing retrieval depth and number of tools
  - Configuring caching of retrieval and intermediate results
  - Selecting appropriate models (smaller vs larger) given context size and complexity
- Considers and optimizes cost drivers:
  - Context length and frequency of calls
  - Use of multiple models or passes in pipelines (retrieve -> summarize -> answer)
  - Trade-off between precomputation (more storage) and dynamic retrieval (more compute)
- Designs fallbacks and degradation strategies:
  - Reduced-context modes under strict limits
  - Graceful messaging when required context cannot be retrieved or included
  - Alternative flows via non-LLM logic for simple queries or high-load situations.","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Safety, Privacy, and Governance in Context Construction","scope_included":"- Identifies and mitigates privacy and security risks arising from context, including:
  - Leakage of sensitive data into prompts and logs
  - Over-broad retrieval across tenants or roles
  - Inclusion of secrets, credentials, or internal-only information where not allowed
- Designs and uses access control enforcement in retrieval and context assembly that respects:
  - User roles and permissions
  - Tenant isolation
  - Data classification labels
- Applies data minimization principles in what is included as context:
  - Only including attributes and fields necessary to complete the task
  - Masking or redacting PII or sensitive content when appropriate
- Implements content filtering in context pipelines:
  - Removing or labeling toxic, biased, or unsafe retrieved content
  - Handling conflicting or low-quality sources with caution
- Designs context and prompts resistant to prompt injection and data exfiltration via retrieved or user-provided content by:
  - Explicitly telling the model how to treat untrusted segments
  - Reasserting policies after untrusted content
  - Avoiding giving models direct instructions from untrusted sources without filtering
- Understands organizational guidelines and regulatory requirements that impact what can be used as context (GDPR, HIPAA-like constraints, internal data policies) and reflects them in design.
- Works with security and compliance stakeholders to define acceptable context sources, retention periods, logging policies, and redaction strategies.
- Designs transparent experiences where the system can show users what sources were used (citations, document IDs) to support auditing and trust, when allowed.","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Systems Integration and Context Pipeline Architecture","scope_included":"- Understands common architecture patterns for context-rich LLM applications:
  - Direct application ↔ LLM calls with simple prompts
  - Application ↔ orchestration layer ↔ retrieval / tools ↔ LLM
  - Microservices or modular pipelines for retrieval, ranking, prompt assembly, and post-processing
- Designs and documents end-to-end context pipelines that specify:
  - Where context originates (systems of record, knowledge bases, logs, APIs)
  - How it is ingested, normalized, embedded, and indexed
  - How it is retrieved, filtered, ranked, and assembled into prompts
  - How outputs are post-processed and stored for reuse
- Integrates with data sources and backends:
  - Relational and document databases
  - Search engines and vector stores
  - Enterprise content repositories and APIs
- Uses and configures LLM orchestration frameworks and libraries to construct pipelines:
  - Flow composition, routing, and branching
  - Tool / function call orchestration and error handling
  - Composition of multiple LLM calls in a single workflow
- Designs interfaces and contracts between components:
  - Structured request/response formats (JSON schemas) for context assembly services
  - Clear expectations for what each service adds to or transforms in the context
- Ensures idempotency and reproducibility of context pipelines where needed (so that evaluations and debugging are consistent).
- Understands where to place caching, logging, and metrics collection within the architecture for effective optimization and monitoring of context behavior.
- Collaborates with other engineers to ensure that context-related components integrate cleanly with authentication, authorization, and existing application services.","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Debugging, Troubleshooting, and Failure Analysis for Context","scope_included":"- Systematically investigates issues where outputs are wrong, unhelpful, or unsafe by checking:
  - What context was actually provided to the model (full prompt inspection)
  - What was retrieved vs what was needed
  - How instructions may have conflicted or been overridden
- Uses logs and traces from orchestration layers, retrieval systems, and LLM calls to reconstruct end-to-end behavior for problem cases.
- Classifies common failure modes:
  - Missing or insufficient retrieval
  - Overly broad/noisy context drowning out key facts
  - Conflicting or ambiguous instructions
  - Misaligned or confusing formatting
  - Latency/cost optimizations that cut out essential context
- Designs and uses debugging tools or views that show:
  - Retrieval results and scores
  - Applied filters and access-control decisions
  - Final assembled prompts with highlighted sections (instructions, context, examples)
- Performs targeted experiments to isolate root causes:
  - Re-running queries with modified retrieval parameters
  - Testing with simplified or alternative prompts
  - Injecting or removing specific context pieces to observe impact
- Distinguishes between issues that should be fixed in context/retrieval/prompting vs those that require model changes, domain data fixes, or business-rule changes.
- Documents discovered failure patterns and feeds them back into design guidelines, test cases, and evaluation sets.
- Establishes incident response practices for serious context-related issues (security/privacy leaks, dangerous advice due to bad context) including temporary mitigations and longer-term fixes.","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Tooling, Platforms, and Infrastructure for Context Engineering","scope_included":"- Works effectively with commonly used tools and platforms that support context engineering, including:
  - Vector databases and similarity search libraries
  - Search engines and hybrid retrieval backends
  - LLM orchestration frameworks and SDKs
  - Embedding-generation services and libraries
- Configures and uses observability and monitoring tools specific to LLM and RAG systems:
  - Logging of prompts, retrieved context, and outputs with appropriate redaction
  - Dashboards for tracking key metrics (latency, cost, error rates, groundedness)
  - Tracing across retrieval, prompt assembly, and LLM calls
- Uses evaluation and experimentation tools that support context-focused experiments:
  - Frameworks for RAG evaluation and LLM-based judges
  - Experiment tracking for different retrieval/prompt configurations
- Automates recurring context engineering tasks where possible:
  - Bulk indexing and re-indexing
  - Embedding re-generation workflows
  - Backfills and migrations for schema or model changes
- Understands deployment and scaling considerations for context services:
  - Sizing and scaling vector/search infrastructure
  - Handling cold starts, cache warmup, and load spikes
  - Designing for high availability of retrieval and context assembly components
- Applies configuration management and versioning to context-related artifacts:
  - Prompt templates
  - Retrieval configurations
  - Index and embedding versions
- Ensures that tooling choices align with organizational standards for security, compliance, and reliability, and can articulate trade-offs between different options.","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Collaboration, Documentation, and Knowledge Sharing for Context Design","scope_included":"- Produces clear, structured documentation for context-related components, including:
  - Architectural diagrams of context pipelines
  - Definitions of context schemas, chunking strategies, and retrieval configurations
  - Prompt templates and their intended behaviors, with rationale
  - Safety and access-control rules applied within context assembly
- Maintains change logs and version histories for prompts, retrieval parameters, and context formats so others can understand when and why behavior changed.
- Works closely with domain experts and stakeholders to:
  - Elicit domain knowledge, policies, and constraints that must be represented in context
  - Validate that context use aligns with domain expectations
  - Prioritize which sources are authoritative
- Participates in code reviews and design reviews with a focus on context-related concerns:
  - Groundedness and correctness
  - Privacy and access control
  - Clarity and maintainability of context logic
- Contributes to shared guidelines and best practices for context engineering within the organization.
- Communicates context-related trade-offs (quality vs cost vs latency vs complexity) to non-technical stakeholders in understandable terms.
- Trains and mentors more junior practitioners on fundamentals of context engineering, including common pitfalls and practical patterns.
- Helps curate internal examples, templates, and reusable components for common context engineering scenarios, while ensuring they are properly generalized and documented.","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Experimentation Workflow and Lifecycle Management for Context Solutions","scope_included":"- Designs iterative workflows for improving context pipelines:
  - Hypothesis formation about what to change (retrieval, chunking, prompts, memory)
  - Controlled experimentation and measurement
  - Rollout and monitoring of changes
- Uses experiment management practices:
  - Tracking versions of prompts, retrieval configs, and index states used in each experiment
  - Comparing multiple candidate configurations on the same evaluation sets
- Plans and executes safe rollouts of context changes:
  - Canary or phased deployments for major context modifications
  - Monitoring for regressions and rollback criteria
- Manages lifecycle of context-related artifacts:
  - Retiring or deprecating obsolete indexes, prompts, and schemas
   - Migrating existing content to new chunking or embedding strategies
  - Handling back-compatibility during transitions
- Coordinates with data engineering, platform, and product teams to schedule large-scale changes that affect indexing, embeddings, or retrieval behavior.
- Ensures that experimental setups reflect real-world conditions:
  - Representative distributions of queries and contexts
  - Inclusion of edge cases and known problematic patterns
- Incorporates feedback loops from production (user feedback, support tickets, incident reports) into context engineering backlogs and evaluation sets.
- Maintains alignment between context engineering choices and broader product and governance roadmaps (data strategy, model strategy, compliance initiatives).","is_non_core_section":true}]}
